text:
Admission Date:  2019-06-18       Discharge Date:  1972-12-26

Date of Birth:   2002-07-17       Sex:  F

Service:  MICU

ADMISSION DIAGNOSIS:  Tracheal stenosis/decreased speaking.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Subglottic stenosis.
2.  Decreased speaking.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:  The patient is a 52-year-old
female with a history of polio, Crohn's disease, breast
cancer, with a history of a tracheostomy placement in
2007-04-16 status post a prolonged hospitalization for
pneumonia with failure to wean at Baptist Health Medical Center - North Little Rock from
2005-02-09 to 1997-10-26.

The patient was transferred to Palomar Health Downtown Campus from 02-21 to 02-21 to evaluate a left
main stem after a rigid bronchoscopy on 05-14 showed
some mild irregularities with a reportedly negative workup.

The patient was originally admitted on 10-27 for medical
management of respiratory failure.  Per the patient, the
patient had been experiencing increased difficulty speaking
and respiratory failure.

Per report, the patient has been experiencing an increased
difficulty in speaking with her Passy-Muir for the past two
to four weeks.  She had noticed an inability to speak for two
to three minutes and "increased pressure."  She also has
increasing pain and irritation around the tracheostomy site
with pruritus and bleeding from the inferior aspect of the
tracheostomy site and the development of blebs and/or
granulation tissue.

The patient denies any other symptoms including fevers,
chills, cough, shortness of breath, nausea, vomiting,
diarrhea, chest pain, abdominal pain, melena, or hematuria.
She notes no emesis or change in mental status.  No increased
production of sputum.  Her tracheostomy has an inner cannula.
At night, the patient goes on a respirator with pressure
support at 2005 and an FIO2 of 0.4.

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1.  Admission from 02-21 to 02-21 for a rigid
bronchoscopy for increased shortness of breath and cough at
Renaissance Hospital Terrell.
2.  Post polio contracted at the age of 17.  Has had spinal
effusion surgery at the age of 9.
3.  Crohn's disease, status post multiple abdominal
surgeries.  Her most recent flare was seven years ago.
4.  Breast cancer in 1997 with a history of lumpectomy and
radiation therapy; currently on Tamoxifen.
5.  Admitted from 07-01 to 10-27 to Kaiser Fnd Hosp - Fontana Hospital with a history of pneumonia; during course
intubated secondary to respiratory failure with failure to
wean.  Percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy tube placed,
drained a pleural effusion.
6.  Tracheostomy in 2007-04-16.
7.  Gastrojejunostomy tube on 2007-04-16; recently
removed chest tube in 2013-04-28.
8.  Medial moderate stenosis of bronchus.

MEDICATIONS ON ADMISSION:  (Medications at home included)
 1.  Combivent.
 2.  Alprazolam.
 3.  Ferrous sulfate.
 4.  Folic acid.
 5.  Fluoxetine.
 6.  Heparin subcutaneously.
 7.  Loperamide.
 8.  Magnesium oxide.
 9.  Ranitidine.
10.  Tamoxifen.
11.  Trazodone.
12.  Tylenol.
13.  Bacitracin.
14.  Simethicone.
15.  Lidocaine.

SOCIAL HISTORY:  She lives with her husband and daughter.  Christina Wilkerson
tobacco or alcohol.  She uses a motorized wheelchair.

FAMILY HISTORY:   She has an aunt with breast cancer and a
parent with colon cancer.

PERTINENT LABORATORY VALUES ON PRESENTATION:  Laboratories on
arrival on 2019-06-18 revealed white blood cell count was
10.6, hematocrit was 32.3, mean cell volume was 86, and
platelets were 339.  Sodium was 137, potassium was 4.3,
chloride was 101, bicarbonate was 38, blood urea nitrogen was
7, creatinine was 0.7, and blood glucose was 101.  INR was
1.2.

PERTINENT RADIOLOGY/IMAGING:  Imaging on 2019-06-18
showed high-grade stenosis of the airway at the level of the
insertion of the tracheal tube, likely extensive granulation
tissue.

A computed tomography of the chest from 2007-06-06
showed trachea course altered secondary to marked
kyphoscoliosis, narrow AP dimension of thorax at the thoracic
inlet, and aberrent calcification of airway noted
particularly left main stem and lower bronchi), dependent
right pleural effusion.

Chest revealed ground-glass opacification, tracheal septal
line, with possible congestive heart failure, slight
narrowing of the left main stem bronchi in region with
exuberant calcifications of the trachea and cartilage, right
upper lobe volume loss and scarring.

A bronchoscopy on 05-14 showed irregularity in the
middle main stem bronchi.

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION ON PRESENTATION:  Temperature was 98.9,
blood pressure was 138/77, heart rate was 96, respiratory
rate was 16, 97% on room air, FIO 2.4.  In general, in no
acute distress.  Her tracheostomy was receiving nebulized
air.  She was short in stature with kyphoscoliosis.  Head,
eyes, ears, nose, and throat examination revealed pupils were
equally round and reactive to light.  Extraocular movements
were intact.  The oropharynx was clear.  Neck with trachea.
The inferior portion of the tracheal site with slight
erythema with clear yellow discharge.  Cardiovascular
examination revealed a regular rate and rhythm.  Lungs with
scoliosis with evidence of a surgical scar along the entire
fascia with spinal stenosis.  Abdomen revealed positive bowel
sounds.  Nontender and nondistended with evidence of vertical
surgical scars.  Extremities were short, contracted, pulses
intact, irregular length.

HOSPITAL COURSE BY PROBLEM:

1.  SPEECH LOSS:  The patient was admitted for evaluation of
speech loss.  A chest computed tomography showed high-grade
stenosis of the airway above and at the lateral insertion of
the tracheostomy tube; likely due to extensive granulation
tissue (performed on 1971-08-18).

On 1987-10-12 a rigid bronchoscopy was performed.  The
rigid bronchoscopy showed complex tracheal stenosis with
slit like lumen located 2.5 cm below the vocal cords with an
estimated length of a few millimeters.  Dilation with
Jacksonian dilators was performed with minimal improvement,
and the recommendation was for Thoracic Surgery to assess for
surgical resection.

2.  INTUBATION:  The patient was maintained on her night
course of pressure support.  A trial of AC was performed;
however, this was not tolerated by the patient.

3.  ANXIETY:  The patient with a history of anxiety.  In
house, she received Ativan 3 mg q.2h.

4.  CROHN'S DISEASE:  The patient with a history of Crohn's
disease.  Her last flare was six years ago.  It was inactive
in house.

5.  BREAST CANCER:  The patient is currently on Tamoxifen for
a history of breast cancer.

6.  PROPHYLAXIS:  The patient was maintained on subcutaneous
heparin.

7.  LINES:  The patient maintained peripheral access.

8.  CODE STATUS:  Full.

DISCHARGE DIAGNOSES:
1.  Tracheal stenosis.
2.  Polio, post polio syndrome.

DISCHARGE STATUS:  Discharge to rehabilitation.

MEDICATIONS ON DISCHARGE:
 1.  ......... 4 mg to 8 mg intravenously q.6h. as needed.
 2.  Albuterol nebulizer solution 1 nebulizer inhaled q.4-6h.
as needed
 3.  Albuterol 1 to 2 puffs inhaled q.4-6h. as needed.
 4.  Ipratropium bromide 2 puffs inhaled q.4-6h. as needed.
 5.  Tamoxifen citrate 20 mg p.o. q.6h.
 6.  Bisacodyl 10 mg p.o. once per day as needed.
 7.  Simethicone 120 mg p.o. four times per day as needed.
 8.  Fioricet one to two tablets p.o. q.4-6h. as needed.
 9.  Lidocaine 5% ointment one application topically twice
per day.
10.  Bacitracin ointment one application topically four times
per day.
11.  Ipratropium bromide nebulizer one nebulizer inhaled
q.4h.
12.  Lorazepam 3 mg p.o. q.2h. as needed.
13.  Oxycodone.
14.  Percocet one to two tablets p.o. q.4-6h. as needed.
15.  Trazodone 300 mg p.o. q.h.s. as needed.
16.  Ranitidine 150 mg p.o. twice per day.
17.  Multivitamin one tablet p.o. once per day.
18.  Loperamide HCL 2 mg p.o. four times per day as needed.
19.  Heparin 5000 subcutaneously q.12h.
20.  Folic acid 1 mg p.o. once per day.
21.  Fluoxetine HCL 20 mg p.o. once per day.
22.  Ferrous sulfate 325 mg p.o. once per day.
23.  Alprazolam 3 mg p.o. q.h.s.

DISCHARGE INSTRUCTIONS/FOLLOWUP:
1.  The patient to receive tracheal care and be ventilated at
night at the outpatient facility.
2.  The patient should have physical therapy and occupational
therapy as needed.



                            [**First Name11 (Name Pattern1) **] Rivera, M.D.
